review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

title: Local History: The Story of My Own Area
subject: History

meta.title: Local History: The Story of My Own Area

slides.title: Local History: The Story of My Own Area
slides.lead: Every street, building and old person near you holds a piece of history. Let's become history detectives and find it!

media.alt: A friendly street scene with a house, a shop, a tree and a signpost showing a street name.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='320' height='52' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='24' y='78' width='70' height='50' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M22 78 L59 50 L96 78 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='44' y='96' width='16' height='32' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='120' y='68' width='80' height='60' fill='#3f6b45'/><rect x='134' y='84' width='52' height='22' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='226' y='60' width='6' height='68' fill='#5c4327'/><rect x='214' y='64' width='40' height='16' rx='2' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='288' cy='58' r='16' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='150' cy='140' r='10' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Warm up by asking learners: what is the name of your street or village? Who is the oldest person you know? Explain that 'local' means the place close to us - our own street, town or area. 'History' means the story of what happened there before we were born. Today we learn four ways to discover that story: landmarks, place names, talking to elders, and museums.

slides.title: What is local history?
slides.lead: Local history is the story of your own area - not far away, but right where you live.

blocks.text: History is not only about faraway kings and big cities. Your own **street, school and town** have a history too. Local history asks: who lived here before us? What did this place look like long ago? How has it changed?

items: Your area was here long before you were born.
Buildings, roads and names carry clues from the past.
You can find the story by looking, asking and visiting.

slides.notes: Make it personal. Ask a few learners to name something old in their area - an old tree, an old shop, a grave, a church, a bridge. Write these on the board. Reassure learners that local history belongs to everyone, in villages and cities alike. Keep language concrete and short at this level.

slides.title: How we find local history
slides.lead: A history detective uses four kinds of clues to learn about an area.

blocks.heading: Landmarks
blocks.text: Old buildings, statues and special places we can see and visit.

blocks.heading: Names
blocks.text: Street and place names that were given long ago for a reason.

blocks.heading: Oral history
blocks.text: Stories that older people remember and tell us out loud.

blocks.heading: Museums
blocks.text: Places that keep old objects, photos and records safe for us.

slides.notes: Introduce the four sources learners will meet in the next slides: landmarks, names, oral history and museums. Ask learners to guess which clue each of their board examples fits. This gives them a simple framework so the lesson is not just a list to memorise. Use the word 'source' - something that gives us information about the past.

slides.title: Landmarks you can see
slides.lead: A landmark is a special place or building that people know well.

blocks.text: Landmarks help us picture the past. An **old church, a war memorial, a bridge, a town hall or a big old tree** can be much older than the people living near it. Looking closely at a landmark - its shape, its stones, the date on it - tells us about the people who built it.

items: Look for a date carved into a wall or a stone.
Notice what a building was used for long ago.
Ask why people chose to build it in that spot.

media.alt: An old stone monument with a plaque showing a date.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='200' height='32' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='72' y='40' width='56' height='90' fill='#b7a98d'/><path d='M70 40 L100 16 L130 40 Z' fill='#9aa77f'/><rect x='84' y='62' width='32' height='24' rx='2' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='88' y='68' width='24' height='4' fill='#cdd6b5'/><rect x='88' y='76' width='24' height='4' fill='#cdd6b5'/></svg>

slides.notes: Take a moment on close observation - a key History skill. If possible, name a real landmark near the school and describe it. Encourage learners to notice details: materials, dates, carvings. Remind them a landmark can be humble, like an old tree or a well, not only a grand monument.

slides.title: Street and place names tell stories
slides.lead: Names are not chosen by accident. Each one has a reason behind it.

blocks.text: Look at the **name of your street, town or river**. Some names come from a language spoken long ago. Some honour a person, a plant, an animal or an event. When a name changes, that is history too - it often shows that the people in charge changed.

items: Some names are in isiZulu, Sesotho, Afrikaans, English or other languages.
Some streets are named after important people.
A new name on an old street can show that times have changed.

media.alt: A green street sign on a pole reading with two arrows.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='104' y='30' width='8' height='110' fill='#5c4327'/><rect x='40' y='44' width='90' height='22' rx='3' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='50' y='52' width='60' height='6' fill='#cdd6b5'/><rect x='96' y='78' width='84' height='22' rx='3' fill='#3f6b45'/><rect x='106' y='86' width='56' height='6' fill='#cdd6b5'/></svg>

slides.notes: Ask learners what language their street or town name comes from and what it might mean. Explain gently that many South African places have been renamed over the years - this is a safe, age-appropriate way to introduce change over time. Avoid heavy politics here; keep it to 'names change when the people in charge change'. [SME] Confirm suitable local place-name examples for the region the class is in.

slides.title: Oral history: stories from elders
slides.lead: Older people carry memories that are not written in any book.

blocks.text: **Oral history** means history that people tell out loud, from memory. Your grandparents, neighbours and elders remember how the area looked, what games children played, and what has changed. When we listen carefully, we learn things no book can tell us.

items: Elders remember shops, schools and roads that are gone now.
Their stories help us picture life before photographs and phones.
Every family and community has its own storytellers.

media.alt: An older person and a child sitting together, the older person speaking.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='72' cy='58' r='18' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='56' y='78' width='32' height='52' rx='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='150' cy='72' r='14' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='138' y='88' width='24' height='42' rx='5' fill='#3f6b45'/><path d='M96 44 h34 a10 10 0 0 1 10 10 v14 a10 10 0 0 1 -10 10 h-18 l-14 12 v-12 h-2 a10 10 0 0 1 -10 -10 v-14 a10 10 0 0 1 10 -10 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain that oral history is a real and valued source used by historians. Stress respect: we listen quietly and say thank you. Point out that in many South African communities, knowledge and history have been passed down by word of mouth for a very long time. Ask learners who the storyteller is in their own family.

slides.title: How to interview an elder
slides.lead: An interview is when we ask someone questions and listen carefully to their answers.

blocks.text: You can collect oral history yourself. Choose an older person, ask politely, and prepare a few **simple questions**. Listen well, and write down or draw what they tell you.

items: Ask nicely first: 'May I ask you about the old days?'
Ask: 'What did our area look like when you were young?'
Ask: 'What has changed the most since then?'
Say thank you, and share what you learned with the class.

slides.notes: This is a practical activity learners can do at home. Model one or two questions aloud. Remind them that some memories can be happy and some can be sad, and that is okay - we listen kindly either way. Encourage them to record answers by writing, drawing or (with permission) recording. Turn this into a homework task if time allows.

slides.title: Museums keep the past safe
slides.lead: A museum is a place that looks after old objects and pictures so we can learn from them.

blocks.text: A **museum** keeps old things - tools, clothes, photographs, letters and maps - safe and on display. Many towns have a small local museum. A **library** can also hold old newspapers and photos of your area. These are wonderful places for a history detective to visit.

items: Museums protect real objects from long ago, called artefacts.
Old photographs show us how a place used to look.
The people who work there can answer your questions.

media.alt: A museum building with pillars and a sign, and a glass display case in front.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='220' height='32' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M40 56 L110 26 L180 56 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='44' y='56' width='132' height='10' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='54' y='66' width='12' height='54' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='84' y='66' width='12' height='54' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='114' y='66' width='12' height='54' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='144' y='66' width='12' height='54' fill='#dcd3c2'/><rect x='44' y='120' width='132' height='8' fill='#b7a98d'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the word 'artefact' - a real object made or used by people long ago. If there is a local museum, library or heritage site, name it and encourage a class visit. Highlight that photographs are a powerful source because they freeze a moment in time. [SME] Add the name and details of the nearest local museum or heritage site for the class's own area.

slides.title: How one place can change

blocks.alt: A timeline showing how an imaginary local area changed from open land to a busy town.

events.label: The area was open land, with a river and a few families.
People built a church, a shop and the first houses.
A school, roads and a small museum were added.
It is a busy area - but the old landmarks are still here.

slides.notes: This is an example timeline, not a real place - use it to teach the idea of change over time, a core Intermediate Phase concept. Invite learners to build a similar timeline for their own area using what elders told them. Point out that some things change (new roads, new names) while some things stay (an old church, a river).

slides.title: Some stories are sad - and important
slides.lead: Not all local history is happy, and that is okay to talk about kindly.

blocks.text: Long ago, unfair laws forced some families to leave their homes and move to new areas. Places like **Sophiatown** in Johannesburg and **District Six** in Cape Town were once busy neighbourhoods, but people were made to move away. Today we remember these stories so we treat everyone fairly.

items: We listen to sad stories with respect and kindness.
Remembering helps us understand our area better.
Learning the truth helps us build a fair country.

slides.notes: Handle this sensitively and briefly at Grade 4 level. Keep it to the simple, true idea that unfair rules once forced families to move, without heavy detail. Sophiatown and District Six are well-known examples with SAHO pages. Reassure learners; focus on remembering and fairness. [SME] Verify the District Six place-page URL slug and confirm the forced-removals framing is pitched correctly for Grade 4.

slides.title: Make your own local history project
slides.lead: Choose one clue and become a real history detective in your area.

blocks.heading: Draw a landmark
blocks.text: Find an old building or place near you and draw it. Write one thing about it.

blocks.heading: Ask an elder
blocks.text: Interview an older person and write down one story they tell you.

blocks.heading: Study a name
blocks.text: Find out why your street, town or river has its name.

slides.notes: Turn the lesson into action. Let learners choose one card as a mini-project to complete at home or over a week. Encourage sharing in the next lesson so the class builds a picture of their area together. This connects the four sources back to real, hands-on work - the heart of the CAPS Local history topic.

slides.title: Review: our own area's story
slides.lead: Local history is all around us - in landmarks, names, elders' stories and museums.

blocks.text: We learned that our own **street, town and area** have a history, and that a history detective can find it using four clues: **landmarks, place names, oral history and museums**. Now try these questions.

blocks.question: Name two things a history detective can look at to learn about their area.
What is oral history, and who can we ask to hear it?
Why is it important to visit a museum or library when studying local history?

slides.notes: Use the three questions as a short written task or class discussion. The marks mirror the simple CAPS style for the Intermediate Phase. Close by reminding learners that history is not only in books - it is in their own street, and they can help keep it alive by sharing what they find. Preview any planned local heritage visit.
